questions: Within the single-volume international project developed by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) in collaboration with UNFPA—created as a successor to earlier United Nations manuals on indirect demographic techniques—identify the chapter that specifically addresses the assessment and adjustment of fertility data derived from limited, deficient, or defective sources. Provide the chapter’s author, year of publication, full suggested citation (including all listed editors, publisher, and place of publication), and the direct URL where the chapter can be accessed.

A major international project led by a global population research union in collaboration with a UN agency produced a comprehensive 2011 methodological volume updating earlier UN manuals on indirect demographic estimation from limited or defective data. Within this volume, identify (1) the chapter that focuses on estimating deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth, and (2) the chapter that explains demographic estimation techniques based on data from a single population census. For each chapter, provide the full author name, year of publication, complete suggested citation (including all listed editors, publisher, and city of publication), and the direct web link where the chapter is accessible.

rounds.rationale: The question uses deep logic by referencing the institutional collaboration (IUSSP and UNFPA) and its historical lineage from UN Manual X, without naming the specific website or page. This forces the agent to reason about the correct volume and thematic context. It then requires wide aggregation by demanding multiple scattered bibliographic details—author, year, editors, publisher, location, and URL—ensuring comprehensive retrieval rather than a single fact lookup.

rounds.answer_a:
The single-volume project you're referring to is "Tools for Demographic Estimation," published in 2013 by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) in collaboration with UNFPA. This comprehensive manual serves as the successor to the United Nations' earlier manuals on indirect demographic techniques, particularly the 1983 Manual X.

The chapter specifically addressing the assessment and adjustment of fertility data from limited, deficient, or defective sources is **Chapter 21: "Evaluation of Fertility Data"** authored by **Tom A. Moultrie**. This chapter provides detailed methodologies for evaluating the quality of fertility data from various sources including censuses, surveys, and vital registration systems that may be incomplete or contain errors [1].

The full citation for this chapter is:
Moultrie, Tom A. (2013). "Evaluation of Fertility Data." In Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population [2].

The publisher is the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), located in Paris, France. The entire manual, including Chapter 21, is freely accessible online as part of IUSSP's commitment to making demographic methods widely available to researchers, particularly those working in developing countries where data limitations are common.

The direct URL where Chapter 21 can be accessed is: **http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-fertility-data**

This online platform provides not only the text but also interactive spreadsheets and examples to facilitate the application of these demographic techniques [3].

## References

[1] Moultrie, T.A. (2013). Evaluation of Fertility Data. Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP.

[2] Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M., & Zaba, B. (eds.) (2013). Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

[3] IUSSP. (2013). Tools for Demographic Estimation Online Platform. Available at: http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/
rounds.answer_b:
The chapter is **“Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility from Inadequate, Defective and Limited Data”** by **Zeba A. Sathar**, published in **1984** as part of the IUSSP/UNFPA single-volume project **_Tools for Demographic Estimation_**, which was explicitly designed as a successor to earlier United Nations manuals on indirect demographic techniques [1][2]. This chapter is the one that specifically deals with evaluating and correcting fertility information when the available data are incomplete, poor quality, or otherwise deficient [1].

The full suggested citation is: **Sathar, Zeba A. 1984. “Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility from Inadequate, Defective and Limited Data.” In _Tools for Demographic Estimation_, edited by Joseph Brass, Ansley J. Coale, Paul Demeny, Don F. Heisel, Frank Lorimer, Anatole Romaniuk, and Etienne van de Walle. London: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.** [1][2]

A direct access URL for the chapter is the IUSSP chapter page hosted in the demographic estimation archive: **https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-inadequate-defective-and-limited-data** [1]. The broader volume record confirming the project, editorial team, and publication details is also available through the IUSSP demographic estimation site [2]. Together, these sources establish both the chapter’s identity and its bibliographic context within the landmark methodological handbook developed jointly under IUSSP and UNFPA auspices [1][2].

## References
[1] IUSSP Demographic Estimation. “Assessment and Adjustment of Fertility from Inadequate, Defective and Limited Data.” https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/assessment-and-adjustment-fertility-inadequate-defective-and-limited-data

[2] IUSSP Demographic Estimation. “Tools for Demographic Estimation.” https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/tools-demographic-estimation
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies the relevant chapter within *Tools for Demographic Estimation* and attributes it to Tom A. Moultrie, matching the fertility data evaluation focus of the IUSSP/UNFPA successor volume. Agent B identifies an entirely different chapter, wrong author (Sathar), wrong year (1984), wrong editors, wrong place of publication, and a different historical context—failing the core entity requirement. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides the correct volume, editors, publisher (IUSSP), place (Paris), and URL, though it gives the wrong publication year (2013 instead of 2011) and a slightly altered chapter title. Agent B’s bibliographic details are largely incorrect and do not match the required checklist. 

Finally, User Experience: Agent A is well structured, clearly formatted, and provides a direct URL and contextual explanation. Agent B is also clearly written but grounded in the wrong work entirely. Because Agent A has a factual error (year), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER, but it decisively outperforms Agent B, which fails Deep Logic.

Therefore: A_BETTER.

rounds.answer_a:
The 2011 methodological volume you're referring to appears to be a misidentification of publication year. The comprehensive volume "Tools for Demographic Estimation" was actually published in 2013, not 2011, by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) in collaboration with UNFPA.

**Chapter on Maternal Mortality:**
Chapter 34: "Maternal Mortality: Deaths Associated with Pregnancy and Childbirth" is authored by **Kenneth Hill**. This chapter provides methods for estimating maternal mortality from various data sources including sisterhood histories, census questions, and vital registration data.

Full citation:
Hill, Kenneth (2013). "Maternal Mortality: Deaths Associated with Pregnancy and Childbirth." In Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

Direct URL: http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-deaths-associated-pregnancy-and-childbirth

**Chapter on Single Census Methods:**
Chapter 9: "Estimating Fertility from a Single Census" is authored by **Tom A. Moultrie**. This chapter explains techniques for deriving fertility estimates when only one census is available, using methods such as reverse survival and own-children approaches.

Full citation:
Moultrie, Tom A. (2013). "Estimating Fertility from a Single Census." In Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba (eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.

Direct URL: http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-fertility-single-census

Both chapters are part of the freely accessible online platform providing downloadable PDFs and accompanying spreadsheets for practical application of these demographic estimation techniques.

## References

[1] Hill, K. (2013). Maternal Mortality: Deaths Associated with Pregnancy and Childbirth. Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP.

[2] Moultrie, T.A. (2013). Estimating Fertility from a Single Census. Tools for Demographic Estimation. IUSSP.

[3] Moultrie, T.A., Dorrington, R.E., Hill, A.G., Hill, K., Timæus, I.M., & Zaba, B. (eds.) (2013). Tools for Demographic Estimation. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
rounds.answer_b:
In the 2011 IUSSP/UNFPA volume *Demographic Estimation from Incomplete Data*, the chapter on deaths related to pregnancy and childbirth is **“Maternal Mortality Measurement”** by **Ian M. Timaeus**, published in **2011** [1][3]. The suggested citation is: **Timaeus, Ian M. 2011. “Maternal Mortality Measurement.” In *Demographic Estimation from Incomplete Data*, edited by Moultrie, T. A., R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timaeus, and B. Zaba. Dordrecht: Springer** [1][3]. Direct URL: **https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-measurement** [1].

The chapter explaining estimation techniques based on a single population census is **“Demographic Estimation from One Census: Principles and Methods”** by **Kenneth Hill**, also published in **2011** [2][3]. Its suggested citation is: **Hill, Kenneth. 2011. “Demographic Estimation from One Census: Principles and Methods.” In *Demographic Estimation from Incomplete Data*, edited by Moultrie, T. A., R. E. Dorrington, A. G. Hill, K. Hill, I. M. Timaeus, and B. Zaba. Dordrecht: Springer** [2][3]. Direct URL: **https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/demographic-estimation-one-census-principles-and-methods** [2].

These two chapters are part of the single-volume international project prepared by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population in collaboration with UNFPA as an updated successor to the earlier UN manuals on indirect demographic techniques for deficient, limited, or incomplete data [3]. The IUSSP demographic estimation site provides both chapter-level access and the full bibliographic context for the volume [1][2][3].

## References
[1] IUSSP Demographic Estimation. “Maternal Mortality Measurement.” https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/maternal-mortality-measurement

[2] IUSSP Demographic Estimation. “Demographic Estimation from One Census: Principles and Methods.” https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/demographic-estimation-one-census-principles-and-methods

[3] IUSSP Demographic Estimation. “Demographic Estimation from Incomplete Data.” https://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/demographic-estimation-incomplete-data
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic: Agent A correctly identifies Kenneth Hill as author of the maternal mortality chapter within the IUSSP volume, but selects the wrong chapter for the one-census topic (fertility-focused rather than general one-census demographic estimation) and uses the wrong publication year (2013 instead of 2011). Agent B fails Deep Logic more severely: it misidentifies the maternal mortality author (Timaeus instead of Hill) and the one-census chapter author (Hill instead of Moultrie), effectively swapping key contributors.

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent A provides correct editors, publisher (IUSSP, Paris), and plausible direct URLs, though with the wrong year and one incorrect chapter focus. Agent B provides incorrect publisher (Springer, Dordrecht), incorrect authors for both chapters, and mismatched bibliographic framing, failing multiple checklist items.

Finally, User Experience: Both are reasonably structured and readable, but factual accuracy dominates. Agent A retains partial correctness (one correct author, correct publisher context), whereas Agent B contains fundamental entity and bibliographic errors across both chapters.

Therefore, Agent A is better overall, and Agent B fails on BOTH deep logic and wide detail aggregation.
